moduleDescription:
Constructive mathematics is naturally typed. -- Simon Thompson

Basic math routines for Nim.

Note that the trigonometric functions naturally operate on radians. The helper functions degToRad and radToDeg provide conversion between radians and degrees.

This module is available for the JavaScript target.

See also

  • complex module for complex numbers and their mathematical operations
  • rationals module for rational numbers and their mathematical operations
  • fenv module for handling of floating-point rounding and exceptions (overflow, zero-divide, etc.)
  • random module for a fast and tiny random number generator
  • stats module for statistical analysis
  • strformat module for formatting floats for printing
  • system module for some very basic and trivial math operators (shr, shl, xor, clamp, etc.)
this func uses bitwise comparisons from C compilers, which are not always available.

entries.name: FloatClass
entries.code:
FloatClass = enum
  fcNormal,                 ## value is an ordinary nonzero floating point value
  fcSubnormal,              ## value is a subnormal (a very small) floating point value
  fcZero,                   ## value is zero
  fcNegZero,                ## value is the negative zero
  fcNan,                    ## value is Not a Number (NaN)
  fcInf,                    ## value is positive infinity
  fcNegInf                   ## value is negative infinity
entries.description: Describes the class a floating point value belongs to. This is the type that is returned by the classify func.

entries.name: isNaN
entries.code: func isNaN(x: SomeFloat): bool {.inline.}
entries.description: Returns whether x is a NaN, more efficiently than via classify(x) == fcNan. Works even with --passc:-ffast-math.

entries.name: signbit
entries.code: proc signbit(x: SomeFloat): bool {.inline.}
entries.description: Returns true if x is negative, false otherwise.

entries.name: copySign
entries.code: func copySign[T: SomeFloat](x, y: T): T {.inline.}
entries.description: Returns a value with the magnitude of x and the sign of y; this works even if x or y are NaN, infinity or zero, all of which can carry a sign.

entries.name: classify
entries.code: func classify(x: float): FloatClass {.raises: [], tags: [], forbids: [].}
entries.description:
Classifies a floating point value.

Returns x's class as specified by the FloatClass enum.

entries.name: almostEqual
entries.code:
func almostEqual[T: SomeFloat](x, y: T; unitsInLastPlace: Natural = 4): bool {.
    inline.}
entries.description:
Checks if two float values are almost equal, using the machine epsilon.

unitsInLastPlace is the max number of units in the last place difference tolerated when comparing two numbers. The larger the value, the more error is allowed. A 0 value means that two numbers must be exactly the same to be considered equal.

The machine epsilon has to be scaled to the magnitude of the values used and multiplied by the desired precision in ULPs unless the difference is subnormal.

entries.name: isPowerOfTwo
entries.code: func isPowerOfTwo(x: int): bool {.raises: [], tags: [], forbids: [].}
entries.description:
Returns true, if x is a power of two, false otherwise.

Zero and negative numbers are not a power of two.

See also:

  • nextPowerOfTwo func

entries.name: nextPowerOfTwo
entries.code: func nextPowerOfTwo(x: int): int {.raises: [], tags: [], forbids: [].}
entries.description:
Returns x rounded up to the nearest power of two.

Zero and negative numbers get rounded up to 1.

See also:

  • isPowerOfTwo func

func `mod`(x, y: float64): float64 {.importc: "fmod", header: "<math.h>",
                                     raises: [], tags: [], forbids: [].}
entries.description:
Computes the modulo operation for float values (the remainder of x divided by y).

See also:

  • floorMod func for Python-like (% operator) behavior

entries.name: round
entries.code: func round[T: float32 | float64](x: T; places: int): T
entries.description:
Decimal rounding on a binary floating point number.

This function is NOT reliable. Floating point numbers cannot hold non integer decimals precisely. If places is 0 (or omitted), round to the nearest integral value following normal mathematical rounding rules (e.g. round(54.5) -> 55.0). If places is greater than 0, round to the given number of decimal places, e.g. round(54.346, 2) -> 54.350000000000001421…. If places is negative, round to the left of the decimal place, e.g. round(537.345, -1) -> 540.0.

entries.name: floorDiv
entries.code: func floorDiv[T: SomeInteger](x, y: T): T
entries.description:
Floor division is conceptually defined as floor(x / y).

This is different from the system.div operator, which is defined as trunc(x / y). That is, div rounds towards 0 and floorDiv rounds down.

See also:

  • system.div proc for integer division
  • floorMod func for Python-like (% operator) behavior

entries.name: floorMod
entries.code: func floorMod[T: SomeNumber](x, y: T): T
entries.description:
Floor modulo is conceptually defined as x - (floorDiv(x, y) * y).

This func behaves the same as the % operator in Python.

See also:

  • mod func
  • floorDiv func

entries.name: euclDiv
entries.code: func euclDiv[T: SomeInteger](x, y: T): T
entries.description: Returns euclidean division of x by y.

entries.name: euclMod
entries.code: func euclMod[T: SomeNumber](x, y: T): T
entries.description: Returns euclidean modulo of x by y. euclMod(x, y) is non-negative.

entries.name: ceilDiv
entries.code: func ceilDiv[T: SomeInteger](x, y: T): T {.inline.}
entries.description:
Ceil division is conceptually defined as ceil(x / y).

Assumes x >= 0 and y > 0 (and x + y - 1 <= high(T) if T is SomeUnsignedInt).

This is different from the system.div operator, which works like trunc(x / y). That is, div rounds towards 0 and ceilDiv rounds up.

This function has the above input limitation, because that allows the compiler to generate faster code and it is rarely used with negative values or unsigned integers close to high(T)/2. If you need a ceilDiv that works with any input, see: https://github.com/demotomohiro/divmath.

See also:

  • system.div proc for integer division
  • floorDiv func for integer division which rounds down.

entries.name: frexp
entries.code: func frexp[T: float32 | float64](x: T): tuple[frac: T, exp: int] {.inline.}
entries.description: Splits x into a normalized fraction frac and an integral power of 2 exp, such that abs(frac) in 0.5..<1 and x == frac * 2 ^ exp, except for special cases shown below.

entries.name: frexp
entries.code: func frexp[T: float32 | float64](x: T; exponent: var int): T {.inline.}
entries.description: Overload of frexp that calls (result, exponent) = frexp(x).

entries.name: splitDecimal
entries.code: func splitDecimal[T: float32 | float64](x: T): tuple[intpart: T, floatpart: T]
entries.description:
Breaks x into an integer and a fractional part.

Returns a tuple containing intpart and floatpart, representing the integer part and the fractional part, respectively.

Both parts have the same sign as x. Analogous to the modf function in C.

If x is empty, 1 is returned.

See also:

  • sum func
  • fac func

entries.name: cumprod
entries.code: func cumprod[T](x: var openArray[T])
entries.description:
Transforms x in-place (must be declared as var) into its product.

See also:

  • prod proc
  • cumproded proc for a version which returns cumproded sequence

entries.name: cumproded
entries.code: func cumproded[T](x: openArray[T]): seq[T]
entries.description:
Return cumulative (aka prefix) product of x.

See also:

  • prod proc
  • cumprod proc for the in-place version

entries.name: cumsummed
entries.code: func cumsummed[T](x: openArray[T]): seq[T]
entries.description:
Returns the cumulative (aka prefix) summation of x.

If x is empty, @[] is returned.

See also:

  • sum func
  • cumsum func for the in-place version

entries.name: cumsum
entries.code: func cumsum[T](x: var openArray[T])
entries.description:
Transforms x in-place (must be declared as var) into its cumulative (aka prefix) summation.

See also:

  • sum func
  • cumsummed func for a version which returns a cumsummed sequence

entries.code: func `^`[T: SomeNumber](x: T; y: Natural): T
entries.description:
Computes x to the power of y.

The exponent y must be non-negative, use pow for negative exponents.

See also:

  • ^ func for negative exponent or floats
  • pow func for float32 or float64 output
  • sqrt func
  • cbrt func

entries.code: func `^`[T: SomeNumber; U: SomeFloat](x: T; y: U): float
entries.description:
Computes x to the power of y.

Error handling follows the C++ specification even for the JS backend https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/numeric/math/pow

See also:

  • ^ func
  • pow func for float32 or float64 output
  • sqrt func
  • cbrt func

entries.name: gcd
entries.code: func gcd[T](x, y: T): T
entries.description:
Computes the greatest common (positive) divisor of x and y.

Note that for floats, the result cannot always be interpreted as "greatest decimal z such that z*N == x and z*M == y where N and M are positive integers".

See also:

  • gcd func for an integer version
  • lcm func

entries.name: gcd
entries.code: func gcd(x, y: SomeInteger): SomeInteger
entries.description:
Computes the greatest common (positive) divisor of x and y, using the binary GCD (aka Stein's) algorithm.

See also:

  • gcd func for a float version
  • lcm func

entries.name: gcd
entries.code: func gcd[T](x: openArray[T]): T
entries.description:
Computes the greatest common (positive) divisor of the elements of x.

See also:

  • gcd func for a version with two arguments

entries.name: lcm
entries.code: func lcm[T](x, y: T): T
entries.description:
Computes the least common multiple of x and y.

See also:

  • gcd func

entries.name: clamp
entries.code: func clamp[T](val: T; bounds: Slice[T]): T {.inline.}
entries.description: Like system.clamp, but takes a slice, so you can easily clamp within a range.

entries.name: lcm
entries.code: func lcm[T](x: openArray[T]): T
entries.description:
Computes the least common multiple of the elements of x.

See also:

  • lcm func for a version with two arguments
